content.text: Before designing an agent, you must answer four questions about its task: how do you
does it operate in, what can it
(Russell & Norvig, 1995) structures these four answers into a single specification.

ensors. Writing a PEAS description before building an agent prevents common mistakes: undefined success criteria, agents that act without observing, or sensors that cannot support the performance goals.

attrs.alt: PEAS Framework diagram: Performance, Environment, Actuators, Sensors with agent in the center
attrs.title: PEAS Framework

content.text: — the criterion by which the agent's behaviour is evaluated. Must be quantifiable: "accuracy ≥ 90%", "latency < 500ms", "zero safety violations". Vague goals like "be helpful" need decomposing.
— everything the agent interacts with but does not control: the physical world, a database, a web page, other agents, users. Includes both the operating context and the source of the agent's inputs.
— the mechanisms through which the agent affects the world: API calls, text output, motor commands, file writes. These are the agent's
— the mechanisms through which the agent observes the world: camera feeds, API responses, user messages, database query results. These are the agent's
, not an implementation pattern. It forces you to answer "what counts as success?" before writing a single line of code — and that question is harder than it looks.
Encoding the PEAS description as a structured object documents the specification and enables automated validation:

from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Any


@dataclass
class PEASDescription:
    """
    Structured PEAS description for an agent task.
    Filling this in before writing any agent code forces clarity
    about what the agent is optimising and what it can observe/do.
    """
    agent_type: str
    # P — Performance measure: what does success look like?
    performance: list[str]
    # E — Environment: what world does the agent operate in?
    environment: list[str]
    # A — Actuators: what outputs / actions can the agent take?
    actuators: list[str]
    # S — Sensors: what inputs can the agent observe?
    sensors: list[str]

    def summary(self) -> str:
        lines = [f"Agent: {self.agent_type}"]
        lines.append("Performance:")
        lines += [f"  • {p}" for p in self.performance]
        lines.append("Environment:")
        lines += [f"  • {e}" for e in self.environment]
        lines.append("Actuators:")
        lines += [f"  • {a}" for a in self.actuators]
        lines.append("Sensors:")
        lines += [f"  • {s}" for s in self.sensors]
        return "\n".join(lines)


# Example: customer support chatbot
chatbot_peas = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Customer Support Chatbot",
    performance=[
        "Issue resolved within 3 turns (rate)",
        "Customer satisfaction score >= 4/5",
        "Escalation rate < 10%",
        "Response latency < 2 seconds",
    ],
    environment=[
        "Live chat interface on company website",
        "Customer's account and order history (database)",
        "FAQ knowledge base",
        "Escalation queue for human agents",
    ],
    actuators=[
        "Send text message to customer",
        "Query order database",
        "Trigger refund workflow",
        "Escalate to human agent",
    ],
    sensors=[
        "Customer text messages",
        "Customer account data (name, order history)",
        "Current conversation history",
        "FAQ document content",
    ],
)
print(chatbot_peas.summary())
The same structure works for very different agent types. An autonomous vehicle has physical actuators and sensors rather than API calls:

autonomous_vehicle_peas = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Autonomous Delivery Vehicle",
    performance=[
        "Packages delivered on time (rate)",
        "Zero accidents or traffic violations",
        "Fuel efficiency (km per litre)",
        "Passenger comfort (smoothness of ride)",
    ],
    environment=[
        "Road network (city streets, highways)",
        "Other vehicles, pedestrians, cyclists",
        "Traffic signals, road signs",
        "Weather conditions",
        "GPS navigation maps",
    ],
    actuators=[
        "Steering control",
        "Accelerator / brakes",
        "Indicators and horn",
        "Navigation system (route update)",
    ],
    sensors=[
        "Camera array (360 degree vision)",
        "LiDAR (depth / obstacle detection)",
        "GPS (position)",
        "Accelerometer / gyroscope (vehicle state)",
        "Radar (speed of nearby objects)",
    ],
)
PEAS for an LLM Code Review Agent
Software agents have the same PEAS structure, but sensors and actuators are entirely digital:

code_review_agent_peas = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="LLM Code Review Agent",
    performance=[
        "True positive bug detection rate >= 80%",
        "False positive rate < 5% (noise reduction)",
        "Actionable suggestion rate (review is useful)",
        "Review latency < 30 seconds per file",
    ],
    environment=[
        "GitHub Pull Request diffs",
        "Repository codebase (context for changes)",
        "CI test results",
        "Team coding guidelines document",
    ],
    actuators=[
        "Post review comment on GitHub",
        "Request changes or approve PR",
        "Create follow-up issue",
        "Run specific linter / test command",
    ],
    sensors=[
        "Git diff for the PR",
        "File content (before and after)",
        "Existing review comments",
        "CI pipeline status and logs",
    ],
)
print(code_review_agent_peas.summary())
A PEAS performance measure must eventually become a
so you can compare agent versions, set acceptance thresholds, and run automated evaluations. Each high-level objective maps to an evaluation function returning a value in [0, 1]:

from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Callable


@dataclass
class PerformanceMeasure:
    """A single measurable objective with its target and weight."""
    name: str
    evaluate: Callable[[dict], float]  # returns 0..1
    weight: float = 1.0               # relative importance
    target: float = 0.8               # minimum acceptable score


def compute_overall_score(measures: list[PerformanceMeasure], outcome: dict) -> float:
    """Weighted sum of individual scores, normalised to 0..1."""
    total_weight = sum(m.weight for m in measures)
    score = sum(m.evaluate(outcome) * m.weight for m in measures)
    return score / total_weight if total_weight > 0 else 0.0


# Define performance measures for the chatbot agent
measures = [
    PerformanceMeasure(
        "resolution_rate",
        evaluate=lambda o: 1.0 if o.get("resolved") else 0.0,
        weight=3.0,
    ),
    PerformanceMeasure(
        "csat",
        evaluate=lambda o: o.get("csat_score", 0) / 5.0,
        weight=2.0,
    ),
    PerformanceMeasure(
        "speed",
        evaluate=lambda o: max(0.0, 1.0 - o.get("turns", 0) / 10),
        weight=1.0,
    ),
]

# Evaluate a sample outcome
outcome = {"resolved": True, "csat_score": 4, "turns": 2}
score = compute_overall_score(measures, outcome)
print(f"Overall performance score: {score:.2f}")  # ~0.87

outcome2 = {"resolved": False, "csat_score": 2, "turns": 8}
score2 = compute_overall_score(measures, outcome2)
print(f"Poor outcome score: {score2:.2f}")  # ~0.19
Three sanity checks are worth automating before you start building:

— can the sensors actually provide the signals needed to measure the performance criteria? If the performance measure is "customer satisfaction" but sensors only provide server logs, you cannot evaluate the agent.
— are the actuators sufficient to achieve the performance goal? An agent with no write permissions in a database cannot fix the data quality issues it finds.
— are all external systems the agent depends on listed? Missing dependencies cause surprises during integration.

def validate_peas(peas: PEASDescription) -> list[str]:
    """
    Sanity checks before building an agent.
    Returns a list of warnings; empty = no issues found.
    """
    warnings: list[str] = []

    if not peas.performance:
        warnings.append("No performance measures defined — "
                         "how will you know if the agent is working?")

    if not peas.sensors:
        warnings.append("No sensors — agent has no way to observe the world.")

    if not peas.actuators:
        warnings.append("No actuators — agent cannot affect the world.")

    # Check that performance can be measured from sensors
    # (heuristic: at least one sensor should relate to the performance domain)
    perf_keywords = set()
    for p in peas.performance:
        perf_keywords.update(p.lower().split())

    sensor_keywords = set()
    for s in peas.sensors:
        sensor_keywords.update(s.lower().split())

    overlap = perf_keywords & sensor_keywords
    if not overlap:
        warnings.append(
            "Performance measures and sensors share no obvious keywords. "
            "Ensure you can actually observe the signals needed to measure success."
        )

    return warnings


# Test: a broken PEAS description
broken = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Misconfigured Agent",
    performance=["customer satisfaction"],
    environment=["website"],
    actuators=[],       # no actuators!
    sensors=["logs"],   # no overlap with "customer satisfaction"
)
issues = validate_peas(broken)
for w in issues:
    print(f"Warning: {w}")
# Warning: No actuators — agent cannot affect the world.
# Warning: Performance measures and sensors share no obvious keywords.
in PEAS deserves special attention because the environment's properties drive architectural choices:
Agent sees the complete world state
Agent sees only a fraction of the world
Same action always produces same next state
Embedding PEAS constraints into the agent runtime ensures the agent cannot call tools outside its actuator list and creates an automatic audit trail for performance evaluation:
Three mistakes appear repeatedly when teams define PEAS for the first time:
— optimising a proxy metric (e.g. clicks) that does not align with the true goal (e.g. user satisfaction).
— the performance goal requires observing something the sensors cannot provide.
— the agent can detect problems but lacks the actuators to fix them, making the performance goal unreachable.

# Pitfall 1: Surrogate performance measure
# The agent optimises a proxy that diverges from the real goal.

wrong_measure = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Content Recommendation Agent",
    performance=[
        # WRONG: clicks are easy to measure but not what we care about
        "Click-through rate >= 15%",
    ],
    environment=["News feed"],
    actuators=["Rank articles"],
    sensors=["User history", "Article metadata"],
)
# Result: agent may learn to promote outrage-bait because it maximises clicks.

better_measure = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Content Recommendation Agent",
    performance=[
        # BETTER: measure actual satisfaction, not proxy behaviour
        "User returns next day (retention rate)",
        "User rates article as useful >= 4/5 (when sampled)",
        "Time-on-site distributes across topics (diversity)",
    ],
    environment=["News feed"],
    actuators=["Rank articles"],
    sensors=["User history", "Article metadata", "Explicit ratings"],
)

# Pitfall 2: Sensor-performance mismatch
# Performance goal requires info the sensors cannot provide.
mismatch = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Medical Adherence Reminder",
    performance=[
        "Patient takes medication on schedule",  # needs sensor to verify
    ],
    environment=["Patient smartphone"],
    actuators=["Send push notification"],
    sensors=["Clock (time of day)"],  # no sensor for actual pill ingestion!
)
# The agent can send reminders but cannot measure whether they worked.
# Fix: add a sensor (pill-dispenser sensor, patient self-report, pharmacy refill data).

# Pitfall 3: Missing actuator prevents goal achievement
limited_agent = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Code Quality Agent",
    performance=["Zero linting errors in repository"],
    environment=["GitHub repository"],
    actuators=["Post comment on PR"],  # can flag but not fix!
    sensors=["PR diff", "Linting results"],
)
# Agent can detect and report errors but cannot fix them.
# Fix: add "Commit code fix" to actuators, or change performance to
# "All linting errors flagged and triaged".
PEAS is not a one-time design exercise. As requirements emerge, the specification evolves. Capturing revisions in code makes the evolution traceable:

from copy import deepcopy


def refine_peas(original: PEASDescription, feedback: dict) -> PEASDescription:
    """
    PEAS is rarely right on the first pass. This function applies
    structured feedback to produce a refined version.

    feedback keys:
        add_performance, remove_performance,
        add_actuator, remove_actuator,
        add_sensor, remove_sensor,
        add_environment, note
    """
    refined = deepcopy(original)
    refined.performance += feedback.get("add_performance", [])
    for r in feedback.get("remove_performance", []):
        refined.performance = [p for p in refined.performance if r not in p]
    refined.actuators += feedback.get("add_actuator", [])
    refined.sensors += feedback.get("add_sensor", [])
    refined.environment += feedback.get("add_environment", [])
    return refined


# Start with a draft chatbot PEAS
draft = PEASDescription(
    agent_type="Customer Support Chatbot v1",
    performance=["Issue resolved"],
    environment=["Chat interface"],
    actuators=["Send message"],
    sensors=["Customer message"],
)

# After a sprint review, the team discovers missing capabilities
v2 = refine_peas(draft, {
    "add_performance": [
        "Customer satisfaction >= 4/5",
        "Escalation rate < 10%",
    ],
    "add_actuator": [
        "Query order database",
        "Trigger refund workflow",
        "Escalate to human agent",
    ],
    "add_sensor": [
        "Customer account data",
        "FAQ document content",
    ],
})
print(v2.summary())
Treat PEAS as a living document. Every production incident that reveals an unconsidered edge case is a signal to extend the
components; every missed goal is a signal to revisit
PEAS provides a four-quadrant specification framework:
nvironment (what does the agent operate in?),

ensors (what can the agent observe?). Completing this specification before implementation prevents ambiguous goals, unreachable performance measures, and missing capabilities. The environment's properties — observability, determinism, episodicity, dynamism — directly determine which agent architectures are appropriate.
